dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess I’ve been thinking about that wedding invitation I got from my cousin. I mean, I just feel... stressed about it. Like, I can’t shake the thought that it’ll be all questions and people I don’t want to see, and it just makes me anxious, you know? I just... I don’t want to deal with how everyone’s gonna look at me or what they’ll say. I already know my mum would have something negative to say about me, and it’s just not worth it. So, I just ignored it. I don’t even know if that’s the right choice, but it felt easier.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like the wedding invitation has brought up a lot of anxiety for you, especially around the expectations and judgments you might face from family. You mentioned feeling stressed and that ignoring the invitation felt easier. Can you tell me more about what specifically causes that anxiety for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Yeah, I mean, it’s just the whole idea of being around family again, you know? I can’t help but think about all the questions they’ll ask—like, “What are you doing with your life?” or “Why haven’t you settled down yet?” It’s like they don’t even see the real me; it’s all surface-level stuff. And then there's my mum, who always has to add her two cents. I just feel like I’m under a microscope, and I don’t want to deal with it. So, yeah, ignoring the invitation felt like the easiest way to avoid all that stress and judgment. I don’t know if that’s healthy or not, but it’s just how I feel.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like being around your family brings up a lot of pressure and a sense of being judged, which understandably contributes to your anxiety. You mentioned feeling like you're under a microscope, and that can be really overwhelming. It's interesting that you recognize ignoring the invitation as a way to cope, even if you're unsure about whether it's a healthy choice. What do you think it would be like to confront those feelings or the situation differently, if you were to consider it?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, honestly. I guess if I tried to confront those feelings, it would probably just make me more anxious. Like, what would I even say? “Hey, I’m here, but I’m super uncomfortable with all of you judging me”? I mean, I feel like I’d just be setting myself up for more stress. And as much as I try to think about it differently, I can’t shake off that feeling of being unwanted or unlovable in those situations. So, it's easier to just avoid it altogether. It’s like, if I don’t go, I won’t have to face all that. I can just... stay in my bubble.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It makes a lot of sense that you would want to protect yourself from those uncomfortable feelings, especially when you associate family gatherings with feeling unwanted or judged. Staying in your bubble feels like a way to keep that anxiety at bay. I'm curious about what it feels like to think about being in that bubble versus the idea of facing the family. What do you think you might be missing out on by choosing to stay away?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess when I think about it, I might be missing out on... I don’t know, maybe some moments that could be nice? Like, I mean, weddings are supposed to be celebrations, right? But then I just picture it going sideways, with everyone buzzing around and me feeling like I’m in the way or something. It’s like, who wants to feel like that? I keep telling myself that avoiding it is the best way to keep myself from feeling bad, but maybe there’s a part of me that wonders if it could be different—if it could actually be enjoyable. But it’s hard to shake off that fear of rejection, you know? It just looms over me, and I can’t help but think it’s better to just avoid it all.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like there's a part of you that recognizes the potential for enjoyable moments at the wedding, but the fear of feeling rejected or uncomfortable feels very strong and protective. It's understandable that you would want to avoid situations that make you feel that way. I'm curious—what do you think it would take for you to feel more at ease in a setting like that, or to see it as more than just a source of anxiety?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I mean, if I really think about what it would take to feel more at ease, I guess I’d have to—like—find a way to block out all those negative thoughts, right? But that’s easier said than done. I just keep imagining what everyone is thinking about me, and it spirals from there. Maybe if I had someone with me, like a friend or something, it would help? But then again, I don't know if I'd even want to put that pressure on someone else. It feels like a lot to ask. So, I don’t know… It just seems simpler to stay away and avoid the whole mess. I don’t know if I’ll ever feel comfortable in those situations.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're feeling caught between wanting to experience the potential joy of the celebration and the weight of those negative thoughts and fears about how others might perceive you. Having a friend there seems like it could provide some comfort, but I can see how it feels like a big ask. It's understandable to want to avoid the mess when it feels overwhelming. I'm curious, though—what do you think it would feel like to explore the idea of bringing someone with you, even just as a thought experiment? What would it mean for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know… thinking about bringing someone with me feels like a double-edged sword. On one hand, yeah, maybe it would help me feel a bit less anxious, like I wouldn’t be completely alone in that sea of judgment. But then I worry about how they’d feel having to deal with me being all tense and awkward. I mean, who wants to babysit someone who’s just uncomfortable the whole time? It would be great if I could just show up and be fine, but I can’t help but think that I’d just drag someone else into my own mess. So, it’s hard to imagine that being a good thing. It just seems easier to keep things simple and avoid the whole situation altogether.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're really weighing the potential benefits of having someone with you against the fear of how your anxiety might affect them. It’s understandable to worry about putting that burden on a friend, especially when you want to handle your feelings on your own. I'm curious—what do you think it would feel like to acknowledge your needs in that situation, both for yourself and for a friend?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I mean, acknowledging my needs sounds nice in theory, but in practice? It feels really complicated. I guess I just don’t want to make things awkward or uncomfortable for anyone else. If I say I need support, it feels like I’m admitting that I can’t handle it on my own, and that’s... I don’t know, it just feels weak to me. I’ve spent so much time trying to convince myself that I can deal with things myself, and it’s like, if I do that, am I just proving to everyone that I’m still that mess? It’s hard to imagine being honest about needing someone there without feeling like I’m just dragging them down with me. So, I don’t know, it’s a tough spot to be in.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of pressure around the idea of needing support, and it’s understandable to want to maintain a sense of independence. It's interesting how you connect needing help with feeling weak or like you're burdening others. Acknowledging your needs can feel complicated, especially if you've been convincing yourself that you should handle everything on your own. I'm curious—what would it look like for you to reframe that idea of needing support? How might it shift your perspective on your own strength?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess, if I try to reframe it, maybe needing support doesn’t have to mean I’m weak? Like, I don’t know, maybe it could mean I’m being smart about it or something? But then again, I just keep going back to that feeling of being a burden. I mean, what if it turns out that I can't handle the situation? It's like I’d be exposing my vulnerabilities, and what if it just confirms all those fears I have about being unlovable or unwanted? I just feel like there's this constant battle in my head between wanting to be strong and not wanting to drag anyone down with me. It's frustrating, honestly. I wish it were simpler.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're navigating a really challenging internal conflict between wanting to show strength and the fear of being vulnerable or a burden to others. The idea of needing support could be seen as a smart choice, but that fear of confirming your worries about being unlovable or unwanted still lingers. It’s understandable to feel frustrated with that complexity. I wonder, is there a way you could start to explore those feelings of vulnerability in a smaller, more manageable way? What might that look like for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, it’s just… everything feels so tangled up in my head right now. Exploring vulnerability in a smaller way sounds like it could work, but I’m not even sure how to start that. I mean, what does that even look like? Maybe just talking to a friend about something small? But then I worry about them not really understanding or just thinking I’m being dramatic. It’s like, even the idea of dipping my toes into that feels risky, you know? I just want to be strong, but then I also feel like I’m stuck in this loop of avoiding everything because of all these fears. It's exhausting.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're feeling really overwhelmed and stuck in a loop between wanting to be strong and the fear of being dramatic or misunderstood. It's understandable to feel hesitant about exploring vulnerability, especially when it feels risky. Talking to a friend about something small could be a step forward, but I can see why it feels daunting. I'm curious—what would it feel like to take just one small step toward that, even if it’s just a thought or a conversation in your mind? How might you begin to shift that fear into something more manageable?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know… it’s just tough to think about. Like, taking a small step sounds nice in theory, but when I picture it, I just get this knot in my stomach. I mean, what if I do try to open up to someone, and it doesn’t go well? What if they think I’m overreacting or just being dramatic? It feels like I’d just be setting myself up for more disappointment. I guess maybe I could just start by acknowledging how I feel, even if it’s just in my own head. But then again, that feels like a slippery slope. What if I can’t handle the feelings that come up? It’s just all so tangled, and I don’t know how to unravel it.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like the idea of taking any step, even a small one, feels really intimidating because of the fear of disappointment or judgment. It’s understandable to feel a knot in your stomach when you think about opening up. Acknowledging your feelings, even just to yourself, seems like a brave first step, but I can see why it also feels like it could lead to more complicated emotions. What do you think might help you feel a little more grounded or safe as you explore those feelings, even in your own mind?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, honestly. I guess feeling grounded sounds nice, but I’m not sure what that even looks like for me. Maybe it’s just finding a quiet space to sit with my thoughts for a minute? But then again, I'm worried about what will come up if I do that. I mean, what if I just spiral down into all those negative feelings? It feels risky to even think about it. I just keep thinking that if I don't confront it, maybe it won't hurt as much. But at the same time, I know that’s not really a solution. It’s like I’m stuck between wanting to feel better and being terrified of what that might mean. It’s frustrating. I wish I could just flip a switch and feel okay about things.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you're feeling really torn between wanting to find a sense of grounding and the fear of what might come up if you allow yourself to sit with those feelings. It's understandable to want to avoid the risk of spiraling into negativity, especially when it feels overwhelming. I can see how frustrating it is to feel stuck between wanting to feel better and the fear of facing those deeper emotions. I'm curious—what would it look like for you to create a safe space for yourself, even if it’s just for a moment, to acknowledge those feelings without judgment? How might you approach that in a way that feels a bit more manageable?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know what that safe space would even look like for me. I mean, I could try just sitting somewhere quiet, but then I start thinking about all the stuff I don’t want to face. It’s like I can feel those feelings bubbling up, and I just want to shut them down before they explode or something. It’s so frustrating because I know avoiding them isn’t the answer, but the thought of sitting with them feels like... like I’m just inviting a storm. I just wish I could figure out how to make it all feel less heavy. I want to find a way to acknowledge what I’m feeling without it turning into a whole mess. But I don’t know how to do that.
